title: Dynamical frustration in space-time metamaterials
authors: Rupesh Mahore, Oleksandr Gamayun, Guillaume Noetinger, Romain Fleury, Corentin Coulais, Benjamin Apffel
abstract: From spin ice and crumpled paper to cold atoms lattices and metamaterials, geometrical frustration occurs generically whenever local constraints cannot be satisfied all at once. The result is a ground state degeneracy, where many equivalent states, each of which contains unsatisfied constraints, coexist. Here, we introduce dynamical frustration, where the ground state degeneracy makes way to a non-reciprocal self-oscillating state instead. To create dynamical frustration, we construct metamaterials that are driven parametrically in time and modulated in space. The parametric pumping leads to period doubling and in turn to a discrete symmetry-breaking. This symmetry breaking, together with the spatial modulation enforces the existence of topologically protected phase dislocations, which propagate unidirectionally with a spontaneous phase that breaks a continuous symmetry. Tesselating 1d frustrated loops, one obtains a 2d metamaterial where phase dislocations self-organize into globally synchronized non-reciprocal phase defects. We expect dynamical frustration to be broadly applicable at any scale, from cold atoms and superconducting circuits to acoustics and RF circuits -- anywhere where space-time modulation can be pushed beyond linear stability.
summary: This work explores dynamical frustration in engineered metamaterials by studying coupled, parametrically driven oscillators. The key finding is that frustration leads to stable, unidirectional phase defects (dislocations) that propagate due to non-reciprocal energy flow. The research validates this theory using both detailed mathematical modeling and comparison with physical experiments.
main_problem: The paper introduces and analyzes 'dynamical frustration,' a phenomenon where ground state degeneracy is replaced by a non-reciprocal, self-oscillating state in driven metamaterials.
main_result: Dynamical frustration leads to the formation of topologically protected, unidirectionally propagating phase dislocations whose velocity is governed by the system's parametric pumping and spatial modulation.
method: The analysis combines theoretical derivations (e.g., multiple scale expansion, continuum limits) with numerical simulations and comparisons to experimental measurements.
model_or_system: The system is modeled using coupled parametric oscillators forming metamaterials, which can be realized in various platforms like cold atoms, superconducting circuits, or mechanical pendulums.
key_observables: Topologically protected phase dislocations, non-reciprocal energy flux, and the dislocation velocity ($\dot{x}_0$).
important_parameters: Parametric pumping amplitude ($\epsilon$ or $P$), spatial winding number ($W$), and coupling strength ($k$ or $\kappa$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on approximations such as the slowly varying envelope approximation and the continuum limit for deriving simplified governing equations.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the concept of frustration via phase states (e.g., on a Möbius strip), show the agreement between simulated and experimental dislocation velocity, and map out parametric resonance instability boundaries (Arnold tongues).
paper_structure: The paper progresses from defining geometric vs. dynamical frustration, deriving the governing equations for coupled parametric oscillators, analyzing the resulting nonlinear wave dynamics (solitons), and finally validating the model against experimental measurements of resonance and defect motion.
why_interesting: It bridges concepts from nonlinear dynamics (solitons, parametric resonance) with condensed matter concepts (frustration, metamaterials), providing a concrete, tunable platform for studying non-equilibrium quantum phenomena.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Concept of dynamical frustration. (a) Parametric pumping of an oscillator and the two subharmonic limit cycles with opposite phase ϕ/2 and ϕ/2 + π. (b) When the pump is shifted by ϕ, the two phase states represented by opposite arrows are tilted by ϕ/2. (c) If the forcing phase is varied from 0 to 2π along a closed chain, the corresponding phase states lie on a Mobius strip due to subharmonic response. If neighboring phase state tend to align due to coupling, a frustration must appear (red line).

FIG. 2. Experimental observation. (a) Sketch of a piece of parametric chain. (b) Chain of coupled parametric oscillators displaying a phase dislocation. Green arrow shows winding of forcing phase. (c) Kinograms at different times showing dislocation propagation. (d) Angular position of oscillators along time showing the propagation of the phase dislocation. (e) A detail of such jump is shown, where both oscillator are initially in phase. The first one decreases its oscillation amplitude until (almost) cancellation at t = 57 s, and starts again with opposite phase. The two oscillators remain out of phase until the second one performs similar phase switch at t = 58.5 s. After the dislocation...

FIG. 3. Model and control of frustration velocity (a) Enve- lope as a function of time and (b) space from direct numeri- cal simulations (DNS) and soliton theory with no adjustable parameter (dashed line). (c) Velocity of the dislocation for W = ±1 as a function of forcing amplitude P from DNS (cir- cles/squares) and theory (dashed line). Dislocation freezes for P > Pc. Inset: SNIC scenario on which attracting (black cir- cle) and repealing (red square) points appear for large enough forcing around each site. (d) Experimental tracking of the dislocation for forcing amplitude of P = 0.65, 1.2 and 1.3. Propagation goes from very regular to complete stopping. (e) Experimental result for...

FIG. 4. Frustration in a two-dimensional network. (a) Tesselation of chains with opposite winding using X-shaped pendulums. (b) Phase state of the experimental lattice at different times. Red line materializes the dislocations, which are synchronized between adjacent cells. (c) Location of both dislocations along time. Regular visit of frustration on the shared edge triggers synchronization. (d) Numerical simulation on 6x6 network with fixed boundary condition, showing global synchronization of the network.

Figure 1: Numerical simulations of the nonlinear equation. (a) Velocity of the frustration for different values of forcing ϵ and coupling k. A clear transition between moving and nonmoving for k = kc(ϵ) is visible. (b) Critical coupling kc(ϵ) above which the frustration moves for different values of N. A linear fit allows to extract kc/ϵ = C(N) (c) Critical ratio C(N) as a function of N.
